questions.text: Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which of the following best describes the scope of prohibited conduct regarding payments to foreign officials?
options: A. Payments to foreign officials are prohibited only if made directly by the company; indirect payments through intermediaries are permitted.
B. Payments to foreign officials for the purpose of obtaining or retaining business are prohibited, including indirect payments through agents or intermediaries.
C. The FCPA applies only to payments made in the United States; payments made outside U.S. territory are exempt.
D. Payments to foreign officials are permitted if approved by the host country's government in advance.
questions.justification_correct: The FCPA prohibits corrupt payments to foreign officials to obtain/retain business, including indirect payments via agents. 15 U.S.C. § 78dd-1, -2, -3. Liability extends to knowing or reckless facilitation through intermediaries.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A ignores agency liability; Option C misrepresents territorial scope; Option D falsely suggests government approval creates exemption. FCPA applies to U.S. persons globally.

questions.text: Under Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 806, which protection is NOT explicitly provided to whistleblowers?
options: D. Protection for internal and external reporting of information reasonably believed to constitute securities fraud.
C. Right to file complaints with OSHA and obtain relief through administrative and judicial remedies.
A. Protection from termination, demotion, suspension, or threatened action for reporting suspected securities violations.
B. Guaranteed financial compensation equal to three years of lost wages and benefits regardless of whistleblower conduct.
questions.justification_correct: SOX § 806 protects from retaliation for reporting; provides remedies including reinstatement and back pay, but does not guarantee fixed three-year compensation. Relief is compensatory, not automatic. 18 U.S.C. § 806.
questions.justification_wrong: Options A, C, D reflect actual SOX protections. Option B falsely guarantees fixed compensation regardless of actual damages or worker conduct, which contradicts remedial nature of statute.

questions.text: Which statement best describes the distinction between wire fraud and bank fraud under federal criminal law?
options: B. Wire fraud involves use of interstate communications for any scheme to defraud; bank fraud specifically involves federally-insured institutions and carries higher mandatory sentences.
A. Wire fraud and bank fraud are identical offenses; the terms are used interchangeably in federal prosecution.
D. Wire fraud requires proof of intent to harm; bank fraud liability is strict and does not require fraudulent intent.
C. Bank fraud applies only to theft from bank accounts; wire fraud covers all electronic communication-based schemes.
questions.justification_correct: Wire fraud: any interstate communication in fraud scheme (18 U.S.C. § 1343; 20 years max). Bank fraud: scheme targeting federally-insured institution (18 U.S.C. § 1344; 30 years max). Bank fraud requires specific intent.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A conflates distinct offenses; Option C mischaracterizes bank fraud scope; Option D falsely states bank fraud is strict liability—both require fraudulent intent and knowledge.

questions.text: Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which of the following payments is explicitly exempted from prohibition?
options: C. Gifts to senior government ministers to secure contracts
B. Payments to foreign officials to obtain business advantages
A. Facilitating payments for routine governmental actions
D. Payments made directly to political parties for influence
questions.justification_correct: 15 U.S.C. § 78dd-1(b) explicitly permits facilitating payments for routine governmental actions (visa processing, permits). These are narrow exceptions for non-discretionary ministerial acts that do not constitute corruption.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, D constitute FCPA violations. Payments for business advantage, gifts to officials, and political contributions are prohibited under anti-bribery provisions regardless of amount or intent.

questions.text: What is the primary legal distinction between wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343) and bank fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1344)?
options: A. Wire fraud requires interstate transmission; bank fraud requires use of federally insured institutions
D. Bank fraud requires actual loss; wire fraud requires only intent to defraud
C. Wire fraud applies to telecommunications only; bank fraud applies to mail and wire
B. Bank fraud carries mandatory minimum sentences; wire fraud does not
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1343 requires interstate/international wire transmission. § 1344 requires scheme to defraud bank or credit union (federally insured institutions). Both require intent and reckless disregard for risk of loss.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B: both statutes carry 20-year maximum; § 1344 has no mandatory minimum. Option C: both apply to wire/mail. Option D: both require intent and risk of loss, not necessarily actual loss.

questions.text: Under Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 806, which statement accurately reflects whistleblower protection scope?
options: B. Retaliation is prohibited only if disclosure occurs through official company channels first
D. Employees must prove actual damages to receive relief under Section 806
A. Protection covers only publicly traded companies and extends to contractors and temporary employees
C. Protection applies exclusively to internal reporting; external disclosures to regulators waive coverage
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 806 protects employees of public companies, contractors, and subcontractors reporting SOX violations. Protection extends to internal and external (regulatory/law enforcement) disclosures, not requiring internal channel exhaustion.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B: internal reporting not mandatory for protection. Option C: external reporting protected. Option D: reinstatement/back pay available without proving damages (burden on employer to prove clear/convincing evidence of legitimate reasons).

questions.text: Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which of the following payments is expressly permitted as a 'facilitating payment' exception?
options: C. Payment to a customs official to overlook violations of import regulations
B. Payment to a foreign official to influence a discretionary business decision regarding contract award
A. Payment to a foreign official to expedite routine governmental action such as processing permits or licenses
D. Payment to a foreign minister to secure a legislative vote favorable to the company
questions.justification_correct: 15 U.S.C. § 78dd-1(b) explicitly permits facilitating payments for routine non-discretionary governmental actions (permits, licenses, utility connections). These are ministerial acts, not policy decisions.
questions.justification_wrong: Discretionary decisions (contract awards, regulatory interpretations, legislative votes) are NOT excepted facilitating payments under FCPA. Payments to influence such decisions constitute violations.

questions.text: Under the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) § 806, which individuals are MOST protected under the whistleblower provisions?
options: B. Employees who report conduct they reasonably believe violates securities laws or internal compliance policies
D. Only shareholders who file formal complaints with the SEC prior to internal reporting
C. Exclusively external auditors and forensic accountants retained by the company
A. Only C-suite executives and audit committee members
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 806 protects employees (including contractors and agents) reporting conduct reasonably believed to violate securities laws or company policies. Broad protection regardless of title or tenure.
questions.justification_wrong: SOX § 806 is not limited to executives, auditors, or shareholders. It applies broadly to employees at all levels who report suspected violations internally or externally.

questions.text: A CFE suspects wire fraud in an international transaction. Which element is MANDATORY under 18 U.S.C. § 1343 (wire fraud statute)?
options: D. Proof that the victim actually suffered financial loss within 30 days of the fraudulent transmission
A. The scheme must involve physical mail in addition to wire communications
B. Use of interstate or foreign wire communication in furtherance of a scheme to defraud or obtain money/property by false pretenses
C. The defendant must be a U.S. citizen or the fraud must originate within U.S. territory
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1343 requires: scheme to defraud, false pretenses, intent to defraud, and use of interstate/foreign wire communications. The wire transmission must further the fraudulent scheme.
questions.justification_wrong: Wire fraud does not require physical mail (distinguishing from mail fraud), immediate victim loss, or U.S. citizenship. Jurisdictional nexus is based on wire communication, not citizenship or location alone.

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a corporate employee reported potential securities fraud internally, but the company retaliated by terminating employment. Which whistleblower protection regime provides federal remedies?
options: B. Section 806 of Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) for public company employees
A. State common law wrongful discharge only
D. International Labour Organization conventions
C. Company whistleblower policies without statutory backing
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1513(e) (SOX § 806) protects employees of public companies reporting internal fraud. Covers reinstatement, damages, attorney fees. Primary federal remedy for securities-related retaliation.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A lacks federal standing. Option C is contractual only. Option D is non-binding. SOX § 806 provides statutory federal protection with remedies before OSHA.

questions.text: In wire fraud prosecutions, what is the minimum intent requirement established in United States v. Namer and related precedent?
options: B. Specific intent to defraud with knowledge of falsity and intent to deprive of property/money
C. General intent only; negligent misrepresentation suffices
A. Strict liability; intent irrelevant once wire communications used
D. Actual monetary loss must be proved by preponderance
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1343 requires specific intent to defraud with scienter. Supreme Court precedent (McNally, Weiner) establishes knowledge of falsity and intent to deprive element.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A violates mens rea requirements. Option C is insufficient; wire fraud requires intent, not negligence. Option D conflates burden with elements; loss must be foreseeable, not necessarily proven.

questions.text: Under the Dodd-Frank Act's whistleblower provisions, which statement regarding the SEC whistleblower award program is correct?
options: A. Awards are limited to internal reporting through corporate compliance channels
D. Awards are disqualified if the whistleblower had prior knowledge of controls
B. Whistleblowers may receive 10-30% of monetary sanctions exceeding $1 million
C. Whistleblowers must disclose violations to their employer before SEC notification
questions.justification_correct: Section 922 of Dodd-Frank (15 U.S.C. § 78u-6) provides awards of 10-30% of monetary sanctions exceeding $1 million recovered by the SEC from enforcement actions.
questions.justification_wrong: Options A, C, D contradict whistleblower protections. Internal-only reporting negates protections; mandatory employer disclosure is not required; prior control knowledge doesn't disqualify awards per SEC regulations.

questions.text: Regarding wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1343, which element is NOT required for criminal prosecution?
options: B. Intent to defraud or obtain money/property by false pretenses
C. Actual financial loss suffered by the victim
D. Use of interstate wire communications in furtherance of the scheme
A. Scheme to defraud involving false pretenses
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1343 requires scheme, intent, and wire use. Actual loss is not an element; attempted fraud qualifies even without financial harm, per U.S. Supreme Court precedent.
questions.justification_wrong: Elements A, B, D are statutorily required. The statute criminalizes the conduct regardless of actual monetary loss incurred; attempts and incomplete schemes still constitute prosecutable wire fraud.

questions.text: Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which of the following scenarios would NOT constitute a violation when a U.S. company makes a payment to a foreign official?
options: C. Payment to a political party in a country where such contributions are legal and properly disclosed
B. Payment made to expedite a routine governmental action such as obtaining a business license
A. Payment of reasonable and bona fide expenditures directly attributable to promoting, demonstrating, or explaining products or services
D. Payment to secure a contract worth $5 million in a high-risk jurisdiction without prior compliance review
questions.justification_correct: The FCPA's Facilitating Payments Exception and the Business Courtesies Defense permit promotional expenditures. 15 U.S.C. § 78dd-1(b) explicitly exempts bona fide promotional costs from anti-bribery prohibitions, provided they are reasonable and properly documented.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, and D violate FCPA. Expedited payments lack the statutory exception (routine governmental action exception was removed). Political contributions to foreign officials raise corrupt intent issues. Large contracts without compliance review breach due diligence obligations.

questions.text: A CFE is retained to investigate suspected wire fraud involving cross-border transfers. Under which federal statute would the examiner primarily establish jurisdiction and elements of the offense?
options: A. 18 U.S.C. § 1343 (Wire Fraud), requiring proof of scheme, interstate wire transmission, and intent to defraud
C. 18 U.S.C. § 1956 (Money Laundering), which criminalizes the scheme initiation itself
D. 18 U.S.C. § 1031 (Securities Fraud), applicable only to listed company securities transactions
B. 18 U.S.C. § 1344 (Bank Fraud), which only applies to deposits and requires no interstate element
questions.justification_correct: Wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1343 requires: (1) scheme to defraud, (2) intent to defraud, (3) foreseeable use of interstate wire communication. Cross-border transfers satisfy the jurisdictional element. This is the foundational statute for interstate fraud schemes per CFE Manual.
questions.justification_wrong: Bank fraud (§ 1344) applies to federally-insured institutions, not all wire schemes. Money laundering (§ 1956) requires predicate fraud; it doesn't establish the scheme. Securities fraud (§ 1031) applies only to securities, not general fraudulent transfers.

questions.text: A whistleblower under Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) Section 806 reports internally suspected securities law violations. The company subsequently terminates the employee for poor performance. What is the examiner's critical analytical focus?
options: C. Determine whether the employee disclosed information to the SEC or law enforcement, which triggers different protection thresholds
D. Verify the termination violated company policy to establish independent grounds for wrongful discharge under state tort law
A. Establish temporal proximity between protected activity (report) and adverse action (termination) to create rebuttable presumption of retaliation under 18 U.S.C. § 1513(e)
B. Prove the company's stated reason for termination is pretextual by demonstrating the employee had no performance deficiencies whatsoever
questions.justification_correct: SOX § 806 (18 U.S.C. § 1513(e)) protects employees reporting suspected violations. Temporal proximity creates rebuttable presumption of retaliation. Examiner must analyze timeline between report and adverse action, then assess employer's articulated reason for pretext. This burden-shifting framework is central to SOX analysis.
questions.justification_wrong: Proving no deficiencies is unnecessarily stringent; pretext analysis permits some performance issues. SEC disclosure triggers different statutory track but doesn't alter § 806 protections. State tort law is secondary; SOX provides federal cause of action with specific remedies and burden allocation.

questions.text: A CFE discovers evidence of wire fraud involving interstate transmission of fraudulent scheme documents. Which federal statute provides primary criminal jurisdiction, and what constitutes the actus reus?
options: A. 18 U.S.C. § 1343; use of interstate wire communication in furtherance of a scheme to defraud
D. 18 U.S.C. § 1347; execution of healthcare fraud involving Medicaid misrepresentation
B. 18 U.S.C. § 1344; making false statements to obtain funds from a financial institution
C. 18 U.S.C. § 1341; use of mails to transmit fraudulent documents across state lines
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1343 defines wire fraud. Actus reus requires: (1) scheme to defraud, (2) intent to defraud/obtain money, (3) use of interstate wire communication. The wire transmission must be in furtherance of the scheme (Pereira v. United States, 347 U.S. 1, 1954).
questions.justification_wrong: § 1344 addresses bank fraud specifically; § 1341 covers mail fraud (different statute); § 1347 covers healthcare fraud. Each has distinct elements and jurisdictional bases. Wire fraud requires interstate wire transmission as essential element, not mails.

questions.text: Under the Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 806, a whistleblower reports internal fraud to the audit committee. After retaliation, the employee files a whistleblower complaint. Which forum has primary jurisdiction?
options: D. Arbitration clause in employment contract takes precedence over SOX protections
B. Department of Labor's Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)
C. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) federal court proceeding
A. State court under common law retaliation doctrine exclusively
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 806 vests jurisdiction in the Department of Labor (OSHA). Employee must file within 90 days of retaliation. OSHA investigates and can order reinstatement, back pay, and damages. Federal jurisdiction preempts state law and arbitration clauses per Burlington Northern v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006).
questions.justification_wrong: Options A, C, and D are incorrect. State courts lack primary jurisdiction; SEC has separate whistleblower programs under Dodd-Frank. SOX § 806 explicitly prohibits enforcement via arbitration agreements per 18 U.S.C. § 806(e)(3)(D). OSHA is exclusive administrative forum.

questions.text: A CFE serving as expert witness in a commercial fraud civil case must meet Daubert standards. Which of the following elements is NOT required for admissibility of expert testimony?
options: B. The expert must have achieved absolute certainty (100%) in his forensic conclusions
C. The testimony must be relevant to a material issue in dispute
A. The expert's methodology must be testable and subject to peer review
D. The expert must disclose the basis and reasoning underlying his opinions
questions.justification_correct: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, 509 U.S. 579 (1993), requires testability, peer review, error rates, and relevance. However, expert conclusions need not achieve absolute certainty; reasonable degree of professional certainty suffices per FRE 702 and case law (United States v. Hankey, 203 F.3d 1160).
questions.justification_wrong: Options A, C, and D are Daubert essentials. Testability, peer review, relevance, and transparent reasoning are mandatory gates for expert admissibility. Absolute certainty is not required; forensic work operates within professional standards of reasonable probability and confidence intervals.

questions.text: Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which of the following payments to a foreign official is NOT prohibited under the anti-bribery provisions?
options: C. A gift to influence a judicial decision in a trade dispute
B. A payment to secure a lucrative oil extraction contract
D. A commission to obtain a government procurement contract
A. A facilitation payment for routine governmental action such as processing permits
questions.justification_correct: Facilitation payments for routine governmental actions (visa issuance, permit processing) are expressly exempted under FCPA §78dd-1(b). These are narrow exceptions for non-discretionary acts.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, and D constitute prohibited payments intended to obtain business advantages through improper influence of foreign officials, violating 15 U.S.C. §78dd-1(a).

questions.text: An expert witness retained by a fraud examiner in a civil litigation matter must understand which primary limitation on expert testimony under Federal Rules of Evidence?
options: C. An expert cannot testify regarding financial fraud unless they hold a CPA license
D. Expert opinions on credibility of witnesses are always admissible if based on experience
A. Expert opinions must be based solely on personal observation and cannot rely on hearsay
B. Expert testimony must be reliable, relevant, and based on sufficient facts or data (FRE 702)
questions.justification_correct: FRE 702 (Daubert standard) requires expert testimony be based on sufficient facts/data, reliable methodology, and relevant to the case. This is the foundational gatekeeping rule for expert admissibility in federal courts.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A ignores that experts may rely on hearsay under FRE 703. C is incorrect; expertise varies. D violates FRE 704(b) prohibition on witness credibility opinions.

questions.text: A CFE investigating wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. § 1343 must establish that the defendant acted with which mens rea requirement?
options: B. Recklessness or gross negligence in transmitting communications
C. Intentional or knowing participation in a scheme to defraud (specific intent)
D. Constructive knowledge that the scheme would likely affect interstate commerce
A. Strict liability without regard to intent or knowledge
questions.justification_correct: Wire fraud requires specific intent: defendant must knowingly execute or attempt to execute a scheme to defraud, with intent to obtain money/property. See United States v. Maze, 414 U.S. 395 (1974); 18 U.S.C. § 1343.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A denies mens rea requirement. B conflates criminal negligence with wire fraud's higher intentional standard. D uses constructive knowledge insufficient for the specific intent requirement.

questions.text: Under the Dodd-Frank Act's whistleblower provisions (SOX 806 and Dodd-Frank 922), which protection is NOT automatically extended to an internal corporate whistleblower?
options: C. Reinstatement and back pay if wrongfully terminated for protected disclosure
A. Protection from retaliation by the employer upon internal reporting of fraud
B. Guaranteed immunity from prosecution for the whistleblower's own criminal conduct
D. Confidentiality of identity when reporting to the SEC under Dodd-Frank procedures
questions.justification_correct: Whistleblower protections do not grant immunity for the whistleblower's own criminal misconduct. SOX §806 and Dodd-Frank §922 protect disclosure rights, not criminal liability of the reporter. See 49 U.S.C. § 42121.
questions.justification_wrong: Options A, C, and D are statutory protections: retaliation prohibition (SOX 806), remedies including reinstatement (29 CFR 1980), and SEC confidentiality rules (17 CFR 240.21F-2).

questions.text: Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which of the following constitutes a prohibited payment that exposes a company to criminal liability?
options: B. Gift of nominal value to a customs official to facilitate routine clearance of legally-owned merchandise
C. Anything of value provided to influence a foreign official's discretionary decision regarding a business advantage
A. Payment of reasonable travel and accommodation expenses for a foreign official to attend a legitimate business meeting
D. Reimbursement of documented meals during a lawful contract negotiation with a state-owned enterprise representative
questions.justification_correct: FCPA § 78dd-1(a) criminalizes payments of anything of value intended to influence foreign officials' official acts to obtain business advantages. The statute's broad 'anything of value' language includes cash, gifts, and benefits intended to corruptly induce official action.
questions.justification_wrong: Options A, B, and D describe permissible facilitating payments or reimbursements explicitly authorized under FCPA's affirmative defenses and routine governmental action exemptions, which do not trigger criminal liability when properly documented.

questions.text: Under Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) § 302, which executive officer certification requirement creates personal criminal liability if knowingly false?
questions.tag: SOX Executive Certification and Criminal Penalties
options: B. Audit committee must certify independence of all external auditors
A. CEO and CFO must certify that financial reports do not contain untrue material statements
D. Board chairman must certify that no material weaknesses exist in disclosure controls
C. General Counsel must certify compliance with all FCPA provisions
questions.justification_correct: SOX § 302 requires CEO/CFO certification that periodic reports contain no untrue material statements and fairly present financial condition. § 906 imposes criminal penalties (up to 20 years) for knowing certification violations, creating direct personal liability.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, and D describe certifications under other statutes or governance frameworks. § 302 specifically targets financial reporting accuracy by principal executives, with criminal sanctions for knowing or willful violations of certification requirements.

questions.text: A CFE is engaged as an expert witness in a federal wire fraud prosecution. Under Federal Rules of Evidence § 702, what is the foundational requirement the examiner must establish to qualify as an expert on forensic accounting methodologies?
questions.tag: Expert Witness Qualification and Evidentiary Standards
options: C. Completion of at least 5 years of continuous employment in a law enforcement agency specializing in financial crimes
D. Testimony must be limited to factual observations and cannot include interpretive conclusions about fraudulent intent
B. Knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education sufficient to provide reliable opinions on accounting fraud detection and financial analysis
A. Possession of a certified public accountant (CPA) license is sufficient to establish expert status without additional qualification
questions.justification_correct: FRE § 702 requires that expert testimony be based on sufficient knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education to reliably assist the trier of fact. CFE credentials, combined with forensic accounting methodology expertise, satisfy this foundational requirement for financial fraud opinion testimony.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A overstates CPA licensure alone; FRE § 702 requires demonstrated expertise in the specific subject matter. Option C imposes unnecessary law enforcement tenure. Option D contradicts § 702's permission for expert opinion interpretation of specialized forensic methodologies.

questions.text: Under the Dodd-Frank Act (SOX § 806), a whistleblower reports internally suspected securities fraud. The employer subsequently terminates the employee. Which legal framework PRIMARILY protects this whistleblower from retaliation?
options: D. The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA), which criminalizes bribery of foreign officials in international business transactions
C. The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA), which regulates financial institution privacy and data security
B. The False Claims Act (FCA) qui tam provisions, which allow private parties to sue on behalf of the government for fraudulent claims
A. Section 806 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX), which prohibits retaliation against employees reporting internally or to government agencies regarding conduct reasonably believed to constitute fraud
questions.justification_correct: SOX § 806 explicitly protects employees reporting suspected securities fraud (including wire/bank fraud schemes) from retaliation. Covers internal reports, SEC filings, and government agency disclosures. Standard remedy: reinstatement, back pay, attorney fees. Normative basis: 18 U.S.C. § 1513.
questions.justification_wrong: FCA addresses fraud against government; GLBA covers privacy (not retaliation); FCPA targets foreign bribery. None provide the specific anti-retaliation protection SOX § 806 guarantees for internal fraud reporting.

questions.text: A CFE is retained as an expert witness in a civil fraud case. The opposing counsel seeks to exclude the expert's testimony regarding financial statement analysis methodology. Under Federal Rule of Evidence 702 and Daubert standard, which factor is MOST CRITICAL for admissibility?
options: A. Whether the expert possesses a relevant professional certification (CPA, CFE, CFA) demonstrating baseline competence
B. Whether the methodology is sufficiently reliable, grounded in established scientific or technical principles, and testable—not speculative conjecture
C. Whether the expert has testified successfully in prior litigation, establishing courtroom experience and credibility
D. Whether the expert's opinion aligns with the hiring attorney's theory of the case, ensuring narrative consistency
questions.justification_correct: Daubert (509 U.S. 579, 1993) requires methodology reliability: testability, peer review, error rates, general acceptance. FRE 702 mandates reliable methods/principles. Certification supports qualifications but insufficient alone; alignment with counsel contradicts independence. Reliability is gatekeeping criterion.
questions.justification_wrong: Certification aids qualification but doesn't ensure methodology reliability. Prior courtroom success is credential irrelevant to Daubert. Alignment with counsel's theory violates expert independence and FRE 702(a) requirement that opinion assist fact-finder, not advocate.

questions.text: A CFE investigating a Ponzi scheme discovers wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343) and bank fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1344) elements. The perpetrator transferred victim funds across state lines and deposited proceeds in accounts bearing false account-holder names. Which statutory requirement(s) are SATISFIED?
questions.tag: Wire Fraud & Bank Fraud - Jurisdictional Elements
options: D. Wire fraud applies only to telephone communications; bank fraud applies only to ATM transactions. Wire transfers and deposit entries do not satisfy statutory definitions.

B. Wire fraud requires interstate wire communication in furtherance of scheme (18 U.S.C. § 1343); bank fraud requires scheme affecting federally insured depository institution (18 U.S.C. § 1344). Cross-state transfers and false-name deposits satisfy both.
A. Wire fraud requires only intent to defraud; bank fraud requires explicit use of false identification. Neither requires interstate commerce or financial institution involvement.
C. Both statutes require proof of actual financial loss; wire fraud caps damages at USD 10,000; bank fraud caps at USD 100,000 per violation.
questions.justification_correct: Wire fraud (§ 1343): scheme to defraud + intent + use of wire communication (mail/wire in interstate commerce). Bank fraud (§ 1344): scheme affecting federally insured institution + intent. Cross-state wire transfers satisfy wire element; false-name deposits satisfy bank element. No damage caps statutory; loss is sentencing factor.
questions.justification_wrong: Wire fraud requires wire communication (not intent alone). Bank fraud requires bank involvement (not false ID alone). No statutory damage caps. Wire transfers constitute wire communication; deposits in federally insured banks satisfy bank fraud. Both require interstate/federal nexus.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a case where a company executive allegedly deprived the employer of the intangible right to honest services by accepting undisclosed kickbacks from a vendor. Under 18 U.S.C. §1346 (Honest Services Fraud), which of the following statements is correct regarding the prosecution of this conduct?
options: A. Honest services fraud under §1346 requires proof of either bribery or kickbacks involving a public official, or a private-sector scheme involving undisclosed self-dealing and kickbacks that defrauds the employer of the intangible right to honest services

B. Following Skilling v. United States (2010), honest services fraud is limited to schemes involving bribery or kickbacks, and does not extend to conflicts of interest or undisclosed self-dealing absent these elements

C. Honest services fraud applies equally to public officials and private-sector employees, requiring only proof that the defendant's conduct deprived the victim of loyal and faithful service regardless of whether bribes or kickbacks were involved

D. The statute requires proof of actual economic harm to the employer in addition to the intangible deprivation of honest services, as established by the Supreme Court's interpretation of the mail and wire fraud statutes
questions.justification_correct: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Skilling v. United States, 561 U.S. 358 (2010), 18 U.S.C. §1346 was narrowly construed to criminalize only schemes involving bribery or kickbacks. The Court rejected broader interpretations that would have encompassed undisclosed conflicts of interest or mere breaches of fiduciary duty. Therefore, to prosecute honest services fraud, the government must prove either bribery or kickbacks—simple undisclosed self-dealing or conflicts of interest, even if they breach fiduciary duties, do not constitute honest services fraud under §1346 post-Skilling.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it suggests honest services fraud extends to undisclosed self-dealing beyond bribery and kickbacks, which contradicts Skilling. Option C is wrong because post-Skilling, mere deprivation of loyal service without bribery or kickbacks is insufficient. Option D is incorrect because honest services fraud involves the intangible right to honest services and does not require proof of actual economic harm—the deprivation of the intangible right itself is the harm.

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a suspected money laundering scheme under 18 U.S.C. §1956(a)(1)(B). The investigation reveals that a corporation conducted financial transactions involving proceeds from wire fraud, with evidence showing the transactions were designed to conceal the nature and source of the funds. Which element must the CFE establish to prove a violation under the concealment prong of §1956(a)(1)(B)?
options: A. The defendant knew the transaction was designed in whole or in part to conceal or disguise the nature, location, source, ownership, or control of the proceeds of specified unlawful activity
B. The defendant intended to promote the carrying on of specified unlawful activity through the transaction
C. The defendant structured the transaction to evade the $10,000 currency transaction reporting requirement under the Bank Secrecy Act
D. The defendant transported or transmitted monetary instruments across state or international borders knowing they represented proceeds of unlawful activity
questions.justification_correct: Under 18 U.S.C. §1956(a)(1)(B), the concealment prong requires proof that: (1) the defendant conducted or attempted to conduct a financial transaction; (2) knowing that the property involved represented proceeds of some form of unlawful activity; (3) knowing that the transaction was designed in whole or in part to conceal or disguise the nature, location, source, ownership, or control of the proceeds; and (4) the property was in fact derived from specified unlawful activity. The specific knowledge requirement regarding concealment intent distinguishes this prong from the promotion prong under §1956(a)(1)(A)(i).
questions.justification_wrong: Option B describes the promotion prong under §1956(a)(1)(A)(i), not the concealment prong. Option C refers to structuring violations under 31 U.S.C. §5324, which is a separate offense from money laundering concealment. Option D describes elements more relevant to §1956(a)(2) regarding international or interstate transportation of monetary instruments, not the concealment prong under §1956(a)(1)(B).

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a publicly traded company subject to SOX §404 requirements. The company's management has prepared an annual assessment of internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR), but the independent auditor has issued an adverse opinion on the effectiveness of ICFR due to a material weakness in revenue recognition controls. Which of the following statements is correct regarding SOX §404 compliance obligations?
options: A. Management's assessment alone satisfies SOX §404(a), but the adverse auditor opinion under §404(b) constitutes a reportable compliance failure that must be disclosed in the Form 10-K
B. The adverse auditor opinion invalidates management's assessment, requiring management to withdraw its assessment and restate prior period financial statements
C. SOX §404 requires only management assessment; the auditor attestation is voluntary for accelerated filers and not required for regulatory compliance
D. The company may cure the §404 violation by implementing a remediation plan within 90 days without disclosure if the material weakness is corrected before the Form 10-K filing deadline
questions.justification_correct: SOX §404(a) requires management to include an internal control report containing an assessment of the effectiveness of ICFR in the annual Form 10-K. SOX §404(b) requires the independent auditor to attest to and report on management's assessment. These are separate but complementary obligations. Management's assessment stands independently and satisfies §404(a) even if deficient controls exist. The auditor's adverse opinion under §404(b) must be disclosed in the same Form 10-K, identifying the material weakness. An adverse opinion is a compliance disclosure, not a violation of the filing requirement itself—both the management assessment and auditor attestation have been completed and disclosed as required.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B is incorrect because management's assessment is not invalidated by an adverse auditor opinion; both reports are disclosed, and the adverse opinion does not automatically trigger restatement obligations (restatements depend on whether the material weakness caused material misstatements). Option C is incorrect because SOX §404(b) auditor attestation is mandatory for accelerated filers and large accelerated filers (smaller reporting companies received exemptions under later rulemaking, but the question addresses publicly traded companies subject to §404 requirements). Option D is incorrect because SOX §404 does not provide a 90-day cure period or permit omission of material weakness disclosure; Item 308 of Regulation S-K requires immediate disclosure of material weaknesses in the Form 10-K, and remediation efforts must be disclosed in subsequent periods until the weakness is remediated.

questions.text: Under the FCPA's anti-bribery provisions, which category of persons would NOT be subject to U.S. jurisdiction for payments made to foreign officials while physically located outside the United States?
options: A. A foreign national employee of a French company with no U.S. operations who made the payment in Germany
B. A U.S. citizen working for a Japanese corporation who made the payment in Tokyo
C. An officer of a U.S. publicly traded company who made the payment while traveling in Brazil
D. A domestic concern that is a privately held U.S. corporation that made the payment in China
questions.justification_correct: A foreign national working for a foreign company with no U.S. nexus falls outside FCPA jurisdiction when acting outside U.S. territory. The FCPA covers U.S. issuers, domestic concerns, and U.S. persons, but does not have extraterritorial reach over foreign nationals of foreign companies with no U.S. connection.
questions.justification_wrong: U.S. citizens (option B) are covered regardless of employer or location. Officers of U.S. issuers (option C) are covered as agents of issuers. Domestic concerns (option D) include all U.S. entities and are covered for acts anywhere in the world.

questions.text: Which element is required to establish a violation of the FCPA's anti-bribery provisions?
options: A. The payment must exceed $10,000 in value
B. The corrupt intent must be to obtain or retain business or secure an improper advantage
C. The payment must actually reach the foreign official
D. The business advantage must actually be obtained
questions.justification_correct: Corrupt intent to obtain or retain business or secure any improper advantage is an essential element of an FCPA anti-bribery violation. The statute requires showing that the payment was made with this corrupt purpose.
questions.justification_wrong: There is no minimum dollar threshold (option A). The FCPA prohibits offers and promises, so actual payment is not required (option C). The statute targets corrupt intent, not actual results, so obtaining the advantage is not necessary (option D).

questions.text: Under the FCPA accounting provisions, issuers must maintain books and records that accurately and fairly reflect transactions in reasonable detail. What standard defines 'reasonable detail'?
options: A. Detail sufficient to detect any transaction exceeding $5,000
B. Detail that would satisfy prudent officials in the conduct of their own affairs
C. Detail requiring documentation of every transaction regardless of amount
D. Detail meeting Big Four accounting firm audit standards
questions.justification_correct: The statutory language and SEC guidance define 'reasonable detail' as a level of detail that would satisfy prudent officials in the conduct of their own affairs. This is the reasonable assurance standard incorporated into the FCPA.
questions.justification_wrong: There is no specific dollar threshold triggering detail requirements (option A). The standard is reasonable, not absolute documentation (option C). While professional standards are relevant, the statutory test is the prudent official standard (option D).

questions.text: When conducting due diligence on a potential third-party agent in a high-risk jurisdiction, which factor would be the LEAST important red flag under FCPA guidance?
options: A. The agent requests payment to an offshore bank account in a third country
B. The agent is related to or closely associated with a foreign official
C. The agent's commission rate is significantly higher than industry norms
D. The agent's company was incorporated within the last five years
questions.justification_correct: Recent incorporation alone is not necessarily a red flag. Many legitimate businesses are recently formed. The other options represent classic FCPA red flags identified in DOJ and SEC guidance.
questions.justification_wrong: Requests for offshore payments (option A), relationships with foreign officials (option B), and excessive commissions (option C) are all specifically identified as significant red flags in FCPA guidance and enforcement actions.

questions.text: Which of the following would qualify as an affirmative defense under the FCPA's anti-bribery provisions?
options: A. The payment was necessary to prevent immediate physical harm to company employees
B. The payment was a reasonable and bona fide expenditure directly related to promotion of products
C. The payment was customary in the foreign jurisdiction and expected by all companies
D. The payment was approved by the company's board of directors
questions.justification_correct: Reasonable and bona fide promotional expenditures directly related to product promotion or contract performance are an explicit affirmative defense under 15 U.S.C. § 78dd-1(c)(2).
questions.justification_wrong: Duress/safety is not a statutory affirmative defense (option A). Local custom is not a defense (option C). Board approval is irrelevant to FCPA liability (option D). The two statutory affirmative defenses are local law and promotional expenses.

questions.text: What is the potential criminal penalty for an individual who knowingly certifies a false Section 302 certification under SOX?
options: A. Fine up to $500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 5 years
B. Fine up to $5,000,000 and/or imprisonment up to 20 years
C. Fine up to $1,000,000 and/or imprisonment up to 10 years
D. Only civil penalties; no criminal liability attaches
questions.justification_correct: Under 18 U.S.C. § 1350 (SOX Section 906), individuals who knowingly certify false statements face fines up to $5,000,000 and/or imprisonment up to 20 years. Willful violations carry these maximum penalties.
questions.justification_wrong: The penalties in options A and C understate the statutory maximums. Criminal liability does attach under Section 906 (option D). The statute provides separate penalties for 'knowing' ($1M/10 years) versus 'willful' ($5M/20 years) violations.

questions.text: Under SOX Section 404(b), which issuers are required to include an auditor's attestation report on internal control over financial reporting?
options: A. All public companies regardless of size
B. Only accelerated filers and large accelerated filers
C. Only companies with market capitalization exceeding $10 billion
D. All public companies except emerging growth companies and non-accelerated filers
questions.justification_correct: Following various exemptions and the JOBS Act, SOX Section 404(b) auditor attestation is required for all public companies except emerging growth companies (EGCs) and non-accelerated filers, which are permanently exempted.
questions.justification_wrong: Not all public companies are required to have auditor attestation due to exemptions for smaller filers (option A). The requirement applies more broadly than just accelerated filers (option B). The threshold is not based on a $10 billion market cap (option C).

questions.text: Under the Bank Secrecy Act, financial institutions must file a Currency Transaction Report (CTR) for currency transactions exceeding what threshold?
questions.justification_correct: The Bank Secrecy Act requires financial institutions to file CTRs (FinCEN Form 112) for currency transactions exceeding $10,000 in a single day. This threshold applies to deposits, withdrawals, exchanges, or other payments.
questions.justification_wrong: $5,000 (option A), $25,000 (option C), and $50,000 (option D) are incorrect thresholds. The statutory and regulatory threshold has been $10,000 since the BSA's enactment, adjusted only for aggregation rules.

questions.text: Financial institutions must file a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) within how many days of detecting facts that may indicate suspicious activity involving potential money laundering or BSA violations?
options: A. 10 calendar days
B. 15 business days
C. 30 calendar days
D. 60 calendar days
questions.justification_correct: Financial institutions must file a SAR within 30 calendar days after the date of initial detection of facts constituting a basis for filing. If no suspect is identified, an additional 30 days is permitted before filing.
questions.justification_wrong: 10 days (option A) and 15 days (option B) are too short and not the regulatory standard. 60 days (option D) exceeds the requirement, though it applies when no suspect has been identified in the initial 30-day period.

questions.text: Under AML Customer Due Diligence (CDD) rules, covered financial institutions must identify and verify the identity of beneficial owners of legal entity customers who own what minimum percentage of equity interests?
options: A. 10 percent or more
B. 25 percent or more
C. 50 percent or more
D. 51 percent or more
questions.justification_correct: The FinCEN CDD Rule requires covered financial institutions to identify beneficial owners who own 25 percent or more of the equity interests of a legal entity customer, as well as one individual with significant managerial control.
questions.justification_wrong: 10 percent (option A) is too low and would be impractical. 50 percent and 51 percent (options C and D) are too high and would miss significant beneficial owners. The regulatory threshold is 25 percent.

questions.text: Under the Bank Secrecy Act, financial institutions must file a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) within how many days after detecting facts that may constitute a basis for filing?
options: A. 30 calendar days from the date of initial detection
B. 60 calendar days from the date of initial detection
C. 15 business days from the date of initial detection
D. 90 calendar days from the date of initial detection
questions.justification_correct: The Bank Secrecy Act requires financial institutions to file a SAR within 30 calendar days after the date of initial detection of facts that may constitute a basis for filing a SAR. This is a fundamental AML compliance deadline.
questions.justification_wrong: 60 days exceeds the required timeframe and would constitute non-compliance. 15 business days uses an incorrect measurement unit (business days rather than calendar days) and incorrect duration. 90 days far exceeds the regulatory deadline and would represent serious non-compliance with BSA requirements.

questions.text: What is the standard of proof required for the government to obtain a civil forfeiture of assets under federal law?
options: A. Preponderance of the evidence
B. Clear and convincing evidence
C. Beyond a reasonable doubt
D. Probable cause
questions.justification_correct: In civil forfeiture proceedings under federal law, the government must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that the property is subject to forfeiture. This is the lowest standard of proof among the civil standards and reflects the non-criminal nature of civil forfeiture.
questions.justification_wrong: Clear and convincing evidence is a higher civil standard used in some contexts but not for federal civil forfeiture. Beyond a reasonable doubt is the criminal standard and does not apply to civil forfeiture proceedings. Probable cause is required to initially seize property but is not the standard for the forfeiture proceeding itself.

questions.text: Under the False Claims Act, what is the minimum whistleblower award percentage if the government intervenes in the qui tam action?
options: A. 15 percent of the proceeds of the action
B. 10 percent of the proceeds of the action
C. 20 percent of the proceeds of the action
D. 25 percent of the proceeds of the action
questions.justification_correct: When the government intervenes in a qui tam action under the False Claims Act, the relator is entitled to receive between 15 and 25 percent of the proceeds, making 15 percent the statutory minimum award.
questions.justification_wrong: 10 percent is below the statutory minimum for intervened cases. 20 percent is within the range but not the minimum threshold. 25 percent is the maximum, not the minimum, for cases where the government intervenes.

questions.text: Under Rule 26 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, what must be included in the initial disclosure without awaiting a discovery request?
options: A. Individuals likely to have discoverable information that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses
B. All individuals with any knowledge related to the subject matter of the litigation
C. Only those witnesses the party definitely intends to call at trial
D. Expert witnesses who will provide testimony on technical matters
questions.justification_correct: Rule 26(a)(1)(A)(i) requires parties to provide the name and contact information of individuals likely to have discoverable information that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment. This is a focused disclosure requirement.
questions.justification_wrong: Disclosing all individuals with any knowledge is overly broad and exceeds Rule 26 requirements. Limiting disclosure only to witnesses definitely intended for trial is too narrow and violates the rule's broader discovery purpose. Expert witnesses are subject to separate disclosure requirements under Rule 26(a)(2) and are not part of initial disclosures under (a)(1).

questions.text: A fraud examiner is analyzing whether charges under 18 U.S.C. § 1957 (engaging in monetary transactions in property derived from specified unlawful activity) can be brought. The defendant received $15,000 in cash from drug proceeds and deposited $8,000 into a bank account, keeping $7,000 at home. Which element distinguishes § 1957 from the related money laundering statute § 1956?
options: A. Section 1957 requires no proof of intent to conceal the nature or source of the funds, only that the transaction exceeded $10,000
B. Section 1957 applies only when the defendant structured transactions to evade reporting requirements
C. Section 1957 requires proof that the transaction was designed to promote further illegal activity
D. Section 1957 necessitates showing the defendant knew the precise criminal offense generating the funds
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1957 requires three elements: (1) the defendant engaged in a monetary transaction, (2) the transaction involved criminally derived property valued at more than $10,000, and (3) the defendant knew the property was derived from unlawful activity. Unlike § 1956, § 1957 requires no specific intent to conceal, disguise, or promote criminal activity—the transaction itself exceeding $10,000 with knowledge is sufficient. This makes § 1957 easier to prove but requires the monetary threshold.
questions.justification_wrong: Structuring to evade reporting is a separate offense under 31 U.S.C. § 5324, not an element of § 1957. Designing transactions to promote further illegal activity is a requirement under § 1956(a)(1)(A)(i), not § 1957, which has no promotional intent element. While § 1957 requires knowledge the property was derived from 'specified unlawful activity,' the defendant need not know the specific offense—general knowledge of criminal derivation suffices under case law.

questions.text: A corporation is defending against charges under the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act (RICO). The government alleges the company committed mail fraud and wire fraud over a seven-year period involving 23 separate fraudulent schemes targeting different victim groups. How many predicate acts must the government prove to establish a "pattern of racketeering activity" under 18 U.S.C. § 1961?
options: A. At least two acts of racketeering activity within a ten-year period
B. At least three acts of racketeering activity showing continuity
C. At least five acts of racketeering activity targeting the same victims
D. At least four acts of racketeering activity within a five-year period
questions.justification_correct: Under 18 U.S.C. § 1961(5), a 'pattern of racketeering activity' requires at least two acts of racketeering activity (predicate acts) committed within ten years of each other, excluding any period of imprisonment. However, the Supreme Court in H.J. Inc. v. Northwestern Bell Telephone Co. established that beyond the statutory minimum of two acts, there must also be proof of 'continuity' and 'relationship.' The statutory baseline remains two acts within ten years, though proving a pattern typically requires demonstrating they are related and constitute a threat of continuing criminal conduct.
questions.justification_wrong: Three acts is not the statutory minimum, though prosecutors often charge more than two to strengthen the continuity showing. Five acts targeting the same victims adds non-existent requirements; RICO does not mandate a minimum of five acts or common victims, though victim patterns may help prove relationship. Four acts within five years imposes both an incorrect number and an incorrect timeframe; the statute specifies two acts and a ten-year window.

questions.text: An attorney is evaluating whether to invoke attorney-client privilege to prevent disclosure of communications during a fraud investigation. The in-house counsel attended meetings where the CFO discussed structuring transactions to avoid regulatory scrutiny, and the attorney provided advice on documentation practices. Under which exception would the privilege most likely be defeated?
options: A. The crime-fraud exception, if there is probable cause that the client was engaged in or planning criminal or fraudulent conduct and the communications were in furtherance of that conduct
B. The business advice exception, when the attorney provided guidance on operational rather than purely legal matters
C. The corporate accountability exception, which prevents corporations from asserting privilege when executives are under investigation
D. The regulatory investigation exception, which nullifies privilege when governmental agencies issue civil investigative demands
questions.justification_correct: The crime-fraud exception defeats attorney-client privilege when a client seeks legal advice to further or conceal ongoing or planned criminal or fraudulent activity. Courts typically require a prima facie showing (often described as probable cause) that (1) the client was engaged in or planning criminal or fraudulent conduct, and (2) the attorney's assistance was obtained in furtherance of that conduct. The privilege does not protect communications made for the purpose of committing crimes or frauds, even if the attorney was unaware of the improper purpose.
questions.justification_wrong: There is no 'business advice exception' that categorically removes privilege; attorney-client privilege can cover business advice rendered in a legal capacity, though non-legal business advice may not be privileged initially. No 'corporate accountability exception' exists; corporations can assert attorney-client privilege, though who controls the privilege in internal investigations raises complex issues. A 'regulatory investigation exception' does not exist; attorney-client privilege generally applies in both civil and criminal contexts, and government investigative demands do not automatically nullify the privilege, though they may be enforceable through court orders if privilege does not apply.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is tracing assets for potential recovery under a civil Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act (RICO) claim. The plaintiff seeks treble damages for losses caused by a pattern of mail fraud. Beyond proving the pattern of racketeering activity, what additional element must the plaintiff establish to recover treble damages under 18 U.S.C. § 1964(c)?
options: A. The plaintiff was injured in his business or property by reason of a violation of Section 1962
B. The plaintiff suffered direct financial loss exceeding the jurisdictional minimum for federal court
C. The defendant's racketeering activity involved an enterprise affecting interstate commerce
D. The plaintiff reported the suspected racketeering activity to law enforcement before filing suit
questions.justification_correct: 18 U.S.C. § 1964(c) provides a private right of action stating: 'Any person injured in his business or property by reason of a violation of section 1962 of this chapter may sue therefor...and shall recover threefold the damages he sustains.' The plaintiff must prove: (1) a violation of § 1962 (the substantive RICO provision), (2) injury to business or property, and (3) causation ('by reason of'). The Supreme Court in Sedima, S.P.R.L. v. Imrex Co. confirmed that injury to business or property and proximate causation are essential elements. Personal injuries are not compensable under civil RICO—only business or property injuries qualify.
questions.justification_wrong: While federal courts have jurisdictional minimums for diversity cases, civil RICO has no specific damages threshold for standing—any injury to business or property caused by a RICO violation suffices, regardless of amount. That the defendant's activity involved an enterprise affecting interstate commerce relates to establishing a § 1962 violation itself, not the plaintiff's standing to sue under § 1964(c); it is an element of the underlying violation, not an additional requirement for civil recovery. There is no requirement to report to law enforcement before filing a civil RICO claim; civil RICO is an independent cause of action available to private parties without any prerequisite of criminal referral or law enforcement notification.
